text: The surgeon closes the fascial defect using a non-absorbable polypropylene V-lock suture. The barbed suture maintains tension along the suture line, and the looped end facilitates starting the suture without knot tying, ensuring a secure closure.

The surgeon uses multi-site robotic instruments with wristed articulation to precisely suture the fascial defect. The needle is carefully placed through the fascia, while the trailing arm maintains tension on the suture line.

The surgeon uses an adequate sized needle with a V-lock suture to take generous bites of the fascia, advancing the suture to close the fascial defect. The barbs on the suture help maintain tension, ensuring the defect remains closed as the suture line progresses along the defect until it is completely closed.
The surgeon releases carbon dioxide to decrease intra-abdominal pressure, facilitating the approximation of the margins of a larger fascial defect during the incisional hernia repair.
The surgeon addresses a hernia along the lateral edge of previously placed mesh, reincorporating the mesh into the current repair process.
The surgeon uses wrist-articulating needle holders to place sutures at various angles, tightening the suture line to prevent gapping between passes.

The surgeon advances the suture line a centimeter or two beyond the apex of the fascial defect. After surpassing the edge, the suture is turned back on itself for additional passes through the fascia. The barbs on the V-lock suture provide resistance, eliminating the need for a knot at the end.
The surgeon makes additional passes of the suture through the fascia in the opposite direction, creating friction that maintains tension on the suture line repair.
The surgeon completes the primary closure of an incisional hernia using a multi-site robotic technique by dividing the suture and removing the needle from the abdominal cavity.
